content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 9 – “P” for PricePart 2 - Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions+ New Product Pricing + Product Mix pricing
Chapter 9 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

9.2 Identify and define the other important external and internal factors affecting a firm’s pricing decisions.
9.3 Describe the main strategies for pricing new products.
9.4 Explain how companies determine a set of prices that maximises the profits from the total product mix.
Learning Objectives for Lecture 9 (1 of 2)

Part 2  - Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions+ New Product Pricing + Product Mix Pricing

Overall marketing strategy, objectives and mix
Marketers must consider the total marketing strategy and mix when setting prices.
Pricing can be used to:
attract new customers or profitably retain existing ones,
deter competitors from entering the market (by setting low prices so that competitors will see the market as not very profitable),
stabilise the market by matching competitors’ prices (avoid a price-war, set prices so that market shares remain about the same),
retain the loyalty and support of resellers,
avoid government intervention (by providing good-value pricing to customers),
create excitement for a brand (by for example providing intro offers or making products affordable that were previously out-of-reach or only affordable as a special “treat” for average consumers).
help the sales of other products in the company’s line (by for example making other products look like better value).
Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (1 of 7)

Overall marketing strategy, objectives and mix (cont.)
Three price-positioning strategies are:
target costing, which starts with an ideal selling price based on “customer-value” considerations and then costs are Managed (usually reduced) so that profit goals can be met whilst charging the “customer-value” price.
non-price positioning, which differentiates the market offering to make it worth a higher price. That is, the product is differentiated on some aspect of the marketing mix other than price, usually on product benefits.
high price positioning, which positions products on high prices.
Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (2 of 7)
Q: Why might a company use high price positioning?

Organisational considerations
Management must decide who within the organisation should set prices.
In small companies, top management often sets prices.
In large companies, divisional or product-line managers set prices.
In industrial markets, salespeople may be allowed to negotiate with customers within certain price ranges.
In industries where pricing is a key factor, pricing departments within the company set prices or help others to set prices.
NOTE: regardless of who does the pricing calculations, and who makes the final pricing decisions, the marketers must ensure that final prices are set such that profit and market share objectives are achieved.
Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (3 of 7)

The market and demand
Pricing in different types of markets
In pure competition, many seller and many buyers, no single buyer or seller has much effect on market price. Example: fruit & veg at the markets.
In monopolistic competition, many sellers and many buyers in the market, and no business has total control over the market price. Products are differentiated from one another (e.g., branding, quality) and hence not perfect substitutes. Example; local restaurants.
In oligopolistic competition (only a few sellers, of products that can be differentiated but not to any great extent), sellers are highly sensitive to each other’s pricing and marketing strategies. Example: supermarkets in Australia
In a pure monopoly (only one seller), pricing depends on whether it is a government monopoly or a regulated monopoly. Example: electricity in Aus.
Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (4 of 7)

Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (5 of 7)
The market and demand (cont.)
Analysing the price–demand relationship
The demand curve is a curve that shows the number of units the market will buy in a given time period at different prices.
Figure 9.4 Demand curve

Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (6 of 7)
The market and demand (cont.)
Price elasticity of demand
Price elasticity is a measure of the sensitivity of demand to changes in price.
Elastic demand refers to the situation where demand changes greatly with changes in price.
Inelastic demand refers to the situation where demand hardly changes with a small change in price.
Q: How might sellers set their prices to respond to elastic demand? To inelastic demand?

The economy
Economic factors (e.g. boom, recession, inflation, interest rates) affect pricing because they affect consumer spending, consumer perceptions of the product’s price and value, and the company’s costs.
Other external factors
The company must consider the impact of its pricing decisions on other parties in the environment, e.g. resellers and the government.
It must also take into account broader societal considerations.
Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions (7 of 7)

Market-skimming pricing
Market-skimming pricing (price skimming) refers to the process of setting a high price for a new product to skim maximum revenue from the segments willing to pay the high price; the company makes fewer but more profitable sales.
Market-penetration pricing
Market-penetration pricing refers to the process of setting a low price for a new product in order to attract a large number of buyers and a large market share.
New-product pricing strategies (1 of 1)
Q: Give examples of companies that use these pricing strategies.

A product’s pricing strategy often has to be changed when the product is part of a product mix.
The company looks for a set of prices that maximises the profits on the total product mix.
Table 9.1 Product-mix pricing
Product-mix pricing strategies (1 of 1)
Pricing situation
Description
Product-line pricing
Setting prices across an entire product line. Includes setting prices within a line of products to direct buyers to the item we most want to sell (probably the highest-margin item), to provide a low-price option to meet competitors’ prices, provide a “premium” priced item to make our mid-priced option look like “better value”.
Optional-product pricing
Pricing optional or accessory products sold with the main product. Accessories can often be priced to yield a higher profit margin whilst keeping the no/fewer options “basic” model at a lower “ticket price”.
Captive-product pricing
Pricing products that must be used with the main product. Example; sell the “driver” item at a low “entry price” (such as the razor or printer) and sell the “slave” items, the “consumables” (razor blades or printing ink cartridges) at a higher-margin price.
By-product pricing
Pricing low-value by-products to get rid of them or to make money on them and the main products. Example: meat off-cuts sold for sausage-making.
Product-bundle pricing
Pricing bundles of products sold together. Example: sell the computer + printer + earphones + second monitor at a “bundle” price which is cheaper the total  price if the buyer were to buy the items separately = better value for the buyer + increased total sales for the seller.

Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions
A: High prices connote* high specification such as luxury, high performance, or containing expensive ingredients. A high price helps to position a product as luxury, high performance etc. To maintain consistency within the Marketing Mix, if a product is to be positioned as luxury/high spec etc. then it must have a high Price, be a luxury/high-specification etc. Product and be sold through Places where luxury/high performance etc. products are usually sold.
(*”connote something (of a word) to suggest a feeling, an idea, etc. as well as the main meaning.” Example: “It's a brand name that connotes luxury.” (https://www.oxfordlearnersdictionaries.com/definition/english/connote?q=connote. 29/9/24)
Q: Why might a company use high price positioning?
A:

Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions
Elastic demand: Raise the price > lower volume at a higher price and therefore have a lower market share. Profit will be higher or lower depending on how total revenue and total costs change at the lower volume level. Final decision depends on management objectives regarding profit and share.
Lower the price > higher volume at a lower price and therefore have a higher market share. Profit will be higher or lower depending on how total revenue and total costs change at the higher volume level. Market share will be higher. Final decision depends on management objectives regarding profit and share.  Dangers of lowering the price: you might lower the brand image/positioning and find it hard to put the price up again. Competitors might lower their prices in return. If price-lowering continues in the market the final position might be little change in market shares but lower profits for all competitors.
Q: How might sellers set their prices to respond to elastic demand? To inelastic demand?

New-product pricing strategies
Market-skimming pricing (price skimming): Each new model of the Apple iPhone is launched at a high price. As that model is superseded by newer models the price of the now-”old” model will be reduced.
Market-penetration pricing: Chinese electric cars on the Australian market. New entrant video-streaming services.
Q: Give examples of companies that use these pricing strategies.

Coming up next
